text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and the surgeon uses a Chelsea Eden anal speculum to visualize the internal opening of the fistula. The fistula tract is curetted to remove granulation tissue. A ureteral catheter is tied to the existing sedum and inserted into the fistula tract to aid in its identification and dissection.

The surgeon palpates and delineates the intersphincteric plane using cautery, followed by a curvilinear incision to access the plane. Four lone star elastic hooks are applied for visualization. Meticulous sharp and blunt dissection is performed using monopolar cautery and a peanut sponge to dissect and identify the intersphincteric tract.

The surgeon performs bilateral dissection near the external sphincter using mixture forceps to navigate around the fistula tract. A right-angled clamp hooks the intersphincteric tract, with the tip directed away from the internal sphincter to prevent perforation. Further dissection is carried out using a peanut sponge and cautery to skeletonize the tract in preparation for ligation.

The surgeon performs transfixed ligation of the tract at the external and internal sphincter openings, tagging the sutures with a small clamp. A number eleven blade is used to divide the tract between the sutures after removing the ureteral catheter. Additional sutures are placed to securely ligate the inter-sphincteric tract stumps at the internal and external openings.

The surgeon ensures adequate hemostasis before loosely closing the incision with interrupted polyglactin 3-0 sutures. The distal part of the tract is cored out to facilitate drainage of the external opening, followed by the injection of local anesthetic to minimize postoperative pain.
